data.body:
[G4 container review — parent 4fcd, child 5a6c `grace-thud-feint` | sibling cross-check: 22bf, a36b] One finding (mode: implicit shared state). The other 6 cross-child modes read clean (details below).

FINDING — Implicit shared state (this child 5a6c ⟷ sibling 22bf, the SIGNED `attest.py` manifest)
- criteria: parent AC1 ("a mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against — e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files"); parent AC3 ("tested: code drift after a signed review → attestation no longer valid").
- location: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` `build_manifest` (lines 27–41) and `sign_plan_review` (lines 56–62) — the deterministic signed manifest; both children write into this exact payload.
- evidence:
  - Confirmed in code: today's `build_manifest` is a flat ordered list (`plan-review: …`, `ticket:`, `material:`, `model:`, `runner:`, `blocking:`, `advisory:`) with NO per-file or per-finding structure (attest.py:33–41); `material_fingerprint` binds DECLARED `file_impact` + child ids, not on-disk content (orchestrator.py:682–689). So neither child's payload exists yet — both must extend this one schema.
  - Sibling 22bf (Story 1) adds a SIGNED `{path: content-hash}` map of the dependency set to `build_manifest`/`sign_plan_review` ("compute a `{path: content-hash}` map … store it inside the signed manifest").
  - This child 5a6c (Story 2) adds, to the SAME signed manifest, per-finding code-hash vintage + the reuse map ("emit a new attestation whose SIGNED payload records each finding's code-hash vintage + the reuse map").
  - The two extensions are on the same function and the same signed blob; 5a6c's chained-attestation vintage scheme is meaningfully defined only ON TOP OF 22bf's content-hash binding (the "code-hash vintage" a finding is stamped with is precisely the dependency-file content-hash 22bf introduces). 5a6c declares `depends_on` 22bf, so the ordering is sound, but the manifest SCHEMA is a shared mutable contract neither AC pins down jointly: 22bf's AC says "binds a SIGNED {path: content-hash} map"; 5a6c's AC says "records per-finding code-hash vintage + the reuse map," and separately "externally CI-verifiable." If 22bf lands its map as a single tree-level hash (it computes `{path: content-hash}` but the AC is silent on whether the per-path entries survive into the manifest verbatim), 5a6c's per-finding vintage chaining has no stable per-file hash to reference.
- scenarios: (a) 22bf serializes its dependency hashes in a form (e.g. one rolled-up Merkle root) that 5a6c's per-finding vintage map cannot index by file → 5a6c must re-open/re-shape 22bf's just-signed manifest schema, an unplanned edit to a sibling's signed-payload contract. (b) Both children independently choose manifest field names/ordering for the new structured payload → divergent signed layouts, breaking the "externally CI-verifiable" guarantee both children assert (signature covers `(ticket_id, manifest)`; any layout disagreement changes the signed bytes).
- impact: The signed-manifest schema is an implicit shared contract across the two stories; without an agreed payload shape (per-file content-hash entries addressable by 5a6c's vintage map, stable field naming/ordering for CI verification), Story 2's chained attestation can require reworking Story 1's signed format after it ships.
- checklist_item: implicit shared state.
- suggested_fix: Add an explicit coordination note pinning the manifest payload contract in 22bf (the owner of the manifest extension): the dependency binding is stored as addressable per-path `{path: content-hash}` entries (not only a rolled-up root) with a fixed field name/ordering, so 5a6c's per-finding code-hash vintage can reference them and the combined manifest stays deterministic/CI-verifiable. Reference that contract from 5a6c's chained-attestation AC.

The other 6 modes (high-confidence, benign-reading filter applied):
- Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN: 5a6c explicitly affirms 22bf's dependency basis ("The dependency basis stays Story 1's proxy set (`file_impact` ∪ citations)") and rejects observed-read-set capture; no contradiction. The reuse-on-code-drift claim that `G3`/`G4`/`BROAD` are code-blind is verified sound — `CODEBASE_GROUNDED = frozenset({"E4","G1G2","A1","G6"})` (registry.py:54) and the orchestrator's only grounding switch keys off exactly this set (orchestrator.py:635–636); no other criterion greps. The version-skew fail-safe correctly guards the one assumption that could silently break this (partition membership changing between sign and re-review).
- Dependency gap — CLEAN: 5a6c declares `depends_on` 22bf; the edge that matters (drift DETECTION provided by Story 1, consumed by Story 2) is present.
- Scope overlap — CLEAN: 22bf explicitly descopes the narrowed delta-re-review ("Out of scope (Story 2): the narrowed delta-re-review on drift. This story always falls back to a FULL re-review"); 5a6c owns exactly that descoped slice. No double-ownership between 5a6c and either sibling. (The known 22bf⟷a36b overlap does not involve 5a6c.)
- Ordering violation — CLEAN: 22bf=Story 1 (detect + full re-review), 5a6c=Story 2 (narrow the re-review). Correct sequence, matched by the dep edge.
- Consumer impact — CLEAN: the claim-path consumer (`claim_gate_check`, attest.py:66–124) reads `ok`/`reason`/`verdict`; 5a6c's chained attestation is a new emission path, not a change to the gate's read contract. No dangling consumer introduced.
- Residual references — CLEAN: 5a6c treats `CODEBASE_GROUNDED` as the authority and explicitly declines to re-derive a parallel code-blind set, so no duplicate/orphaned partition definition is introduced.
